---
title: "Food allergy in a changing dietary landscape: A focus on the Asia Pacific region"
author: "Leung ASY, Pacharn P, Tangvalelerd S, Sato S, Pitt E, Wong G et al."
year: 2024
doi: "10.1111/pai.14211"
journal: "Pediatric Allergy and Immunology"
volume: "35(8)"
pmid: "DON'T KNOW"
access: "Paywalled — Wiley Online Library (403 on fetch)."
source_quality: "Search result summaries only. Full text not accessed."
---

# Food allergy in a changing dietary landscape: A focus on the Asia Pacific region

**Authors:** Leung ASY, Pacharn P, Tangvalelerd S, Sato S, Pitt E, Wong G et al.
**Year:** 2024
**DOI:** 10.1111/pai.14211
**Journal:** Pediatric Allergy and Immunology, 35(8)

## Why this paper

Brought in for EuroPrevall data specific to Indian centres. EuroPrevall (a large multi-country food allergy prevalence study) is one of the few studies that collected systematic data from Indian sites (Mysore and Bengaluru). This paper synthesises Asia-Pacific patterns — useful for situating Indian data regionally.

## Extracted points

> **Source quality note:** All points from search result summaries. Full text not accessed.

1. EuroPrevall INCO study recruited 35,549 children aged 6–11 from China (Hong Kong, Guangzhou, Shaoguan), Russia (Tomsk), and India (Mysore and Bengaluru). — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Context: This is a cross-sectional survey on food allergy prevalence.
   For us: The two Indian sites are Mysore (Karnataka) and Bengaluru — both in southern India. Findings may not represent northern or north-eastern India.

2. Food allergy prevalence from EuroPrevall INCO (from search summary, comparing sites):
   - Hong Kong: 1.50%
   - Guangzhou: 0.21%
   - Rural Shaoguan: 0.69%
   - India (combined): 0.14%
   — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Context: DON'T KNOW how food allergy was defined/confirmed in this study. DON'T KNOW whether the 0.14% for India is the same figure cited in P01 and P04 or a separately derived number.
   For us: India has the lowest prevalence figure across all EuroPrevall INCO sites. Urban-rural comparison within China suggests environment matters.

3. Most prevalent allergen in China: shrimp. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Most prevalent allergen in Russia: fish. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Eggs: consistently reported as common in young children across Asia Pacific. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Shellfish: prevails among older children across the region. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   For us: The dominant allergens differ by country. India's dominant allergens from this study are DON'T KNOW.

4. Rise of wheat and tree nut allergies observed in Asia, in contrast to peanut allergy rise in the West. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Context: Attributed to changes in dietary habits (Westernisation), increased urbanisation, economic growth.
   For us: Relevant for the trajectory argument — India's allergen profile may shift as diet changes.

5. Regional shifts linked to urbanisation and dietary Westernisation. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   For us: This is a stated hypothesis in the paper, not a measured finding — flag appropriately in manuscript.

## Gaps and challenges the authors document

DON'T KNOW — full text required.

## Questions for full text access

- What specific allergens did the EuroPrevall INCO study identify at the Mysore and Bengaluru sites?
- Were there differences between the two Indian cities?
- What definition of food allergy was used (oral food challenge vs. self-report + sensitisation)?
- Do the authors comment on foods not covered by standard allergen panels in the Indian context?
